sections.title: Chairman's Chat
sections.content:
Tam (AKA Glorious Leader) reports

Learning to Swim. In the deep end. In a pool full of treacle.

We have an issue. A big issue. “Drone Observation Of Clouds and their role in weather and climate”. It’s an academic research project looking at the dynamic and complex nature of clouds - with the aim of delivering  “a transformative dataset for atmospheric research”. You can read about it here. Sounds great I can hear you say - we’re always interested in clouds and their development and behaviour - the more knowledge the better, surely. So what’s the issue?  Well, the issue is that the company doing the research, Menapia, have identified Snaizeholme in the valley below Dodd Fell as the spot they want to launch their drone from. They have applied for an Airspace Change Proposal (ACP-2025-053), to establish a 1km Temporary Danger Area ((T) DA) in front of the Dodd Fell top launch, which would be activated by NOTAM. IF activated for flying days that would suit Dodd, it would effectively close the site.

Dodd has for some years been one of our prime sites, having taken most of the XC hounds away from Wether due to the better ground directly downwind. The site record at 193.8km is the highest XC distance from a Dales site - remarkably set (by Dean) nearly 12 years ago, surely ripe for beating!  Losing access to the site on good flying day would be a terrible blow for the club.  The ACP has just entered the “Stakeholder Engagement” phase, which rightly names the club as a key stakeholder.  This phase ends on March 27. By then we need to respond to the proposal.  Our engagement with Menapia started with Simon Hulme (our airspace cmte member) and Rob Urselmann (safety) meeting Paddy Logan (Menapia CEO) on Feb 23 to point out the impact of the proposal on the club. As I write, it appears like the meeting was constructive, and Menapia were showing a commitment to work with us to gain a mutually agreeable solution. Fingers crossed by the time of publication of this newsletter we will know better. Key dates coming up are the Cmte meeting on 19th March at which point we will finalise the clubs response and let everyone know in time for the deadline of March 27. As with everything else, the website will hold the formal documents.

As if that wasn’t enough, whilst part way through writing the above, we received notice that the Leeds Bradford ACP (ACP-2021-066) has now also just entered the Stakeholder Engagement phase, and responding to it will undoubtedly dwarf any effort that we have and are putting into the Snaizeholme proposal. It is early days for further work on this, it will be around for a while, but a number of us will be getting up to speed on the history and re-establishing the alliances that will be needed to respond. If you have time and interest you can also catch up by reading what’s on the forum here. We often remark that the forum is never looked at - to put the threat that the LBA ACP poses into some sort of context, this topic runs to 19 pages on the website and has been visited 178,000 times! Time to buckle up.

Fly safe everyone.

sections.title: Letters from the Sofa
sections.content:
Joseph Edmonds, Newsletter Editor reports

So as my Dad, John, wrote about last month and Pete is mentioning below, I learned the hard way that getting legs up early can bit a bit of a pain in the ass. I landed hard on my bum from about 5m up, onto rock hard ground. I could feel the generous padding of my Impress 4 harness soak up the bump, but the “jerk” effect was clearly felt as it went up my spine to a certain point where it stopped. An instant “ooh that doesn't feel good” and I quickly and clearly shouted for help as I got the wing under control. I knew it wasn’t good. Turns out Indian hospitals are pretty good.

Anyway, I guess that’s probably my flying season cooked for this year. Hopefully others in the 10 100 group can pick up the baton and shepherd another round of hopefuls up to the magic 3 digit KMs and beyond in 2026.

Seeing as I now have not a great deal to do, I’ve finally got around to sprucing up the newsletter system. You probably wont really notice much difference, but if you spot any bugs please let me know. Main thing is that you can now view the backlog of issues and I don’t have to resort to any shorturl hacks etc. It’s a much better system and also much easier to maintain going forwards.

https://www.dhpc.org.uk/skywords/

sections.title: Coaching
sections.content:
Pete Logan, Chief Coach reports

For such a good flying year in ‘25 we did well to have few or no incidents to speak of. A bit of winter travel has brought two incidents that we can learn from. Both pilots will be fine, which was a relief to hear.

The first was a last moment avoidance and then contact with telegraph power / lines which resulted in a very heavy landing. This is mainly an issue landing out in a strange field rather than the normal bottom landing but CPs should take note since conditions can dictate you not making an official and clear bottom landing. We pass on to each other the danger of lines but rarely the best way to spot them. Straight things stick out in a rural environment from many hundreds of metres, so a telegraph pole is really what you’re looking for. Use your imagination to paint in the lines between them.

If it's unavoidable or you've realised too late then the standard advice remains. Get your legs down early and be ready to PLF / brake your fall, mush in to land early if possible, turn away and land cross or downwind if not, and land well. What do I mean by landing well? In this case, excess speed is the issue. Keep your hands up, speed is your friend. A big flare with a wrap right before you contact the ground allows you to scrub off most speed and lessen any impact.

Of course, this is best avoided by not getting into these situations. When I'm close to the ground and not intending to land I have a landing field in mind plus a backup option. In selecting those I've looked for livestock, crops and hazards. Perhaps a new mnemonic is needed? I propose LAATTCH:

Livestock

Access (paths, gates, tracks)

Approach (constant aspect, S turns)

Telegraph (poles, lines)

Turbulence (lee side of structures & trees)

Crops

Hazards (people, undulations, water, bumpy surface)

You'll remember Laattch on the gate as you'll undo it to exit the field. Or send in your better suggestion and I'll put it in next month.

The second incident involved a bit of bad luck on take off. Conditions were thermic when the pilot took off and a few seconds later they had a frontal which developed into an 80% asymmetric deflation. They descended the five or so metres without the collapsed side reinflating and so landed heavily in the pod harness. It's rare to see such a huge piece of turbulence come through. The pilot was already getting into the harness very shortly after they took off which meant that their legs were out of the way and not available for PLF or as a crumple zone.

It's good practice to treat your take offs in phases; Launch the wing, Check the wing, Accelerate to TO, Stabilise flight, Get comfortable - now's the time to get your legs up and adjust straps. BTW, big wriggles or holding risers to get into your harness is going to be adding a sharp weight shift to your wing early in flight and indicates your harness setup isn't right. Spend some time in a harness hanger to sort out your strap tension, seat angles, pod length etc.

We could probably add a letter to the TO safety mnemonic - Will Geordie Have His Cat Aboard Today. Something like "Lads/Lasses"for Legs. That's the least lame I could think of. Any better suggestions, you know where to reach me.

Who knew Geordie was a Hindu deity with occasional lateral thumb malformation issues?

sections.title: Sites and News
sections.content:
Tom Shuttleworth (Sites North)

Spring means lambs!

[Yes, it’s A.I. I’m afraid]

At certain times of the year, we need to be particularly mindful of the needs and wants of those with whom we share the land and sky.  Lambing season is one of them.  There’s a section on lambing in the excellent article by Christina Richards ‘Sheep Farming and Free Flight’ in April 2025’s Skywords.  She identifies a number of useful things we need to consider, and a couple of sentences stand out for me:

  • very pregnant ewes should not be made to run
  • sheep farmers at this time are understandably absolutely frazzled

As always, please check the site guides for any particular restrictions or requests.

sections.title: Proposal for drone flying TDA at Dodd Fell.
sections.content:
Simon Hulme (Airspace Officer) & Rob Urselmann report

A Temporary Danger Area (TDA) smack in our soaring space on Dodd Fell during prime flying season. Sounds very ominous…..

However, we have already had in-person discussions and if all turns out as agreed in principle, there should be no or very little impact on our flying at Dodd.

Area of the TDA overlaid on the PG soaring heatmap from thermal.kk7.ch (approximate)

There's an Airspace Change Proposal in progress with the CAA to fly a Beyond Visual Line Of Sight (BVLOS) drone at Dodd Fell this summer for research project “Drone Observation Of Clouds (DOOC) and their role in weather and climate”. It’s an academic research project looking at the dynamic and complex nature of clouds - with the aim of delivering “a transformative dataset for atmospheric research”. You can read about it here.

3. Overview of the Proposed Trial

Location: 6km South-West of Hawes, Yorkshire Dales National Park

( 54° 15′ 52.16′′ N, longitude 2° 15′ 47.77′′ W (54.264489, -2.263269)

Airspace Type: Temporary Danger Area (TDA)

Lateral Extent: 1 km radius

Vertical Extent: Surface to 7,000 ft AMSL

Activity: BVLOS vertical profiling drone flights (DJI Matrice 350 RTK - MTOW 9.3kg)

Nature of Operations: Predominantly stationary or vertical ascents/descents. 5-6 flights of 30-40 mins with target altitude up to 1,600 m AGL for cloud penetration; collecting atmospheric particle data.

Activation: Limited periods, notified by NOTAM only when required

Duration: Temporary trial for a defined period - 25 June to 30 November 2026, Monday - Friday. 0900-1700 local (daylight hours only).

Descent: From max operating alt 6000' it takes the drone 6 minutes to descent in safe mode.

The location puts the TDA smack in our soaring space on Dodd Fell during prime flying season, which sounds very ominous.

Options:

We really only have two options:

1)  object to the trial due to the impact to our operations in our submission to Stage 3 engagement and leave it to the CAA to adjudicate or

2) try to reach a compromise.

Having discussed with Chair Tam, we took the second approach at our meeting but it was without prejudice so if after working out details the result is unfavourable to DHPC we can still object.

Engaging promptly and constructively, Simon Hulme (our newly appointed airspace officer) and Rob Urselmann (safety officer) visited in person with the TDA Proposer, Menapia, in Leeds on 25 Feb.

Key points:

Location choice: Menapia's long term ambition is to use the Warcop Range danger area but the MOD agency can’t meet timelines  due to key position vacancies. Snaizeholme is a 'fall-back' to enable the research to proceed this season and the university happened to have some contacts at the Woodland Trust who own the land.

25 June trial start is the earliest DOOC flying date based on an approval on 18 May.  If they start on time the trial will likely be complete in September.  If the clearances don’t dome through in time the trial start will slip and be aligned with AIRAC cycles (internationally agreed 28 day cycle for publishing airspace changes).  The end date is the end date based on the end of the weather window, i.e. 30 Nov latest.

Operations: Whilst going only vertical and with a human operator right there, the drone (UAV) operation is classed BVLOS because a) it ascends above 400ft and b) it will penetrate clouds to sample from. The drone flies a near dead-vertical profile within a tiny 15m radius cylinder.  If the drone strays outside 50m they must immediately land. The 1km TDA radius was stipulated as absolute minimum by the CAA, so no point arguing that.

Weather: The objective of the research is to study convective clouds, which are predominantly formed in polar maritime and returning polar maritime airmasses so SW through NW wind directions  Drone operations are constrained to a max wind of 10m/s (36kph) so in effect we will be looking for the same thermic weather and wind conditions for both our flying and the DOOC trial.  The operational overlap cannot be removed only managed.  Menapia are well aware of the potential impact of their trial on our flying and have been very willing to come to a workable compromise.  

It is not yet clear if the trial airspace will be a temporary danger area (TDA) or temporary restricted airspace (TRA); either way it will be activated by NOTAM and since the 48 hour submission window for a NOTAM is much longer than our likely flying decision  the NOTAM will show the airspace as permanently active.  However, we have agreed that when the forecast wind is in the window 260-325 (Dodd Fell wind window +10 either side) they will not operate later than 1000 Local.  This is a significant compromise on Menapia’s part and on our side we need to accept that we may lose a little bit of flying on the margins of the wind window. There are very very limited flights logged from Dodd Fell before 1100 so hopefully the 1000 L DOOC cease fly will not be an issue for us.

We still have to operationalize the plan with Menapia and gain CAA approval but the intent is for a telephone hotline and website URL to be listed on the NOTAM.  The hotline will have a voice message giving the status of the TDA/TRA for the day - it will be imperative that all pilots call this hotline to check the status of the TDA/TRA before flying. If the trial airspace is active, pilots must not fly from Dodd Fell - if the TDA/TRA is infringed they will have to cease flying and submit an occurrence report.  We aim to have the status confirmed by 0800 L and they have agreed to show the current weather at Dodd Fell from their base station.

We have discussed with Menapia the possibility of shifting the TDA as far North as possible; e.g. moving 2km up the valley opens up 90% of the Dodd ridge without penetrating the TDA area; however, this may not be achievable due to a host of their safety constraints but they will examine if it’s possible. That said if we all comply with the plan as it stands we should lose little if any flying and we shouldn’t be infringing an active Avoid.

As already mentioned there is work to do to operationalise the plan and Menapia are working hard to get all their clearances in place but we think we have a good plan.  We remain closely engaged with Menapia as well as the RSAG (Regional Soaring Airspace Group) and local PG clubs.  We will be submitting formal feedback as part of the CAP 1616 process by 27 March.

sections.title: Life Membership for Trev
sections.content:
Tam reports

Shortly after our club was established in 1974 a young engineer named Trevor Birkbeck moved to Yorkshire. Soon after that he witnessed hang gliders flying at Sutton Bank. Interest was sparked. Then one day, working at Emley Moor, he met a group of pilots who were meeting up to go and try out some new site.  It was early days for Hang Gliding and kit was rudimentary, often home made and, well, dangerous. Intrigued, Trev started mythering the assembled pilots. He didn’t get much response until he started on a certain Noel Whittal, who turned out to be a fellow motorbike enthusiast (both can be seen in the video celebrating the clubs first 25 years, on Youtube). The group invited him along to see the day’s action. Although Noel’s intentions were certainly honest and helpful, it is almost certainly just a coincidence that he shortly afterwards sold Trev his first wing.  Thus began a flying career that lasted decades, spanned the world and has only recently seen Trev finally hang up his flying suit and accept that time has caught up with him and his ever youthful outlook. A legend and stalwart not only of this club, but also hang gliding nationally, if you ever get the chance sit him down and ask about the early days and his career - it will be several hours well spent! . When the club honoured Martin at the AGM with an award for the serious tenure as Chair covering 16 years, we confidently predicted that it would never been beaten - not realising that it already had been - Trev was Chair for 17 years (possibly spread over 2 different periods, but he can't remember!). In addition to this he has been on the committee in various posts for very many years, most recently as hang gliding representative, of course. In short, he has been a kingpin of the club since most of us were at primary school, or even before we were born for the younger guns!  

Trev’s undiminished enthusiasm for flying was admirably shown simply by his attendance at the club night in early February.  A talk on Richard Butterworth’s successful flying year in 2025 - a talk on paragliding. Trev was there , still interested in expanding his knowledge and hearing of successful flights - and he doesn’t even fly hang gliders any more, never mind paragliders!

I will forever be enormously proud that the first decision that the new committee made after I became chair was to award Trev life membership, there will never be an easier decision for us. It was definitely a humbling experience making the award, one of those moments where you just feel inadequate.  

Congratulations Trev, an incredible contribution to the club and the wider sport in general.

(this award was prepared for the Jan club night, but neither Trev nor Tam could make it to the night!!)

sections.title: Early Warning - New Treasurer Required
sections.content:
Tam / Davey B

At the AGM next December (!) Dave is going to stand down as Treasurer, a post he has held for 6 years.  Treasurer is clearly a key post in the club, and required by our constitution.  We will therefore need someone to step forward and fill Dave’s boots. You do not need to be a qualified accountant, just be organised and willing to put something back into the club.  Membership of the cmte does not need to be an onerous task, and all manner of things get discussed at meetings, which can if necessary be attended remotely, Committee meetings are held 5 times a year, plus the AGM.  We are advertising this post early, as there would be an opportunity to shadow Dave for the rest of the year, so that you are already familiar with exactly what’s required in the role before you actually take over in December. If anyone would like a chat to find out a bit more about the role and how it all works you can contact  Tam (chairman@dhpc.org.uk) or Dave (treasurer@dhpc.org.uk) for an initial chat.

sections.title: Safety
sections.content:
Rob Urselmann reports

A ton of effort this month went into the Snaizeholme TDA (see above) and no doubt will continue in March.

Fly safe, and please stay well away from the newborn lambs and high-pregnant sheep for their safety and wellbeing.
